aqi.advice_good: Air quality is satisfactory — it's a great day to be active outside
aqi.advice_moderate: Unusually sensitive people should consider reducing prolonged or intense outdoor activity
aqi.advice_unhealthySensitive: Sensitive groups should reduce prolonged or intense outdoor activity | Take more breaks and watch for symptoms such as coughing or shortness of breath
aqi.advice_unhealthy: Everyone should reduce prolonged or intense outdoor activity | Sensitive groups should avoid outdoor exertion | Consider moving activities indoors
aqi.advice_veryUnhealthy: Everyone should avoid prolonged or intense outdoor exertion | Sensitive groups should avoid all outdoor physical activity | Move activities indoors or reschedule
aqi.advice_hazardous: Health warning — everyone should avoid all outdoor physical activity | Stay indoors and keep activity levels light | Follow tips for keeping particle levels low indoors

seo.whatIsAqi: What is the Air Quality Index (AQI)?
seo.whatIsAqiAnswer: The Air Quality Index (AQI) is a tool developed by the <epa>U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)</epa> to communicate how clean or polluted the air is and what health effects may be of concern. The AQI runs from 0 to 500 and is divided into six color-coded categories: Good, Moderate, Unhealthy for Sensitive Groups, Unhealthy, Very Unhealthy, and Hazardous.
seo.howToRead: How do I read the AQI values?
seo.howToReadAnswer: Lower AQI values mean cleaner air. An AQI of 0–50 (green) is Good, 51–100 (yellow) is Moderate, 101–150 (orange) is Unhealthy for Sensitive Groups, 151–200 (red) is Unhealthy, 201–300 (purple) is Very Unhealthy, and 301–500 (maroon) is Hazardous. When the AQI exceeds 100, air quality is considered unhealthy — at first for sensitive groups, then for everyone as the AQI rises.
seo.sensitiveGroupsAnswer: Sensitive groups include people with heart or lung disease, older adults, children and teenagers, people who are pregnant, and people who are active outdoors. These groups are more likely to be affected when air quality is poor. If you are in a sensitive group, you can reduce your exposure by taking fewer and shorter outdoor walks when the AQI is elevated.
seo.pollutantsAnswer: The AQI covers five major pollutants regulated under the Clean Air Act: ground-level ozone (O₃), particle pollution (PM₂.₅ and PM₁₀), carbon monoxide (CO), sulfur dioxide (SO₂), and nitrogen dioxide (NO₂). An AQI value is calculated for each pollutant, and the highest value determines the overall AQI.
seo.dataSource: Where does the air quality data come from?
seo.dataSourceAnswer: The hourly air quality data comes from the <cams>Copernicus Atmosphere Monitoring Service (CAMS)</cams>, which provides global atmospheric composition forecasts. The data is accessed via <openmeteo>Open-Meteo</openmeteo>, which provides free, open-source weather and air quality APIs. The AQI categories and health guidance follow the <epa>EPA AirNow</epa> technical standard (EPA-454/B-24-002).
seo.healthEffects: What are the health effects of poor air quality?
seo.healthEffectsAnswer: Poor air quality can cause respiratory issues, aggravate asthma and allergies, and increase the risk of cardiovascular problems. Fine particles (PM₂.₅) can penetrate deep into the lungs and bloodstream. The <who>World Health Organization</who> estimates that air pollution causes millions of premature deaths worldwide each year.
seo.forecastUpdate: How often is the air quality forecast updated?
seo.forecastUpdateAnswer: The <cams>CAMS</cams> air quality forecast is updated regularly using atmospheric models. Weather data such as temperature and cloud cover comes from <openmeteo>Open-Meteo</openmeteo>. The forecast provides up to 5 days of hourly air quality predictions.
seo.sources: AQI categories and health guidance: EPA AirNow (EPA-454/B-24-002, May 2024). Air quality data: CAMS/Copernicus via Open-Meteo.

disclaimer.heading: Disclaimer
disclaimer.general: This website is for informational purposes only and does not constitute medical advice. Air quality data and health guidance are approximate and should not replace professional medical advice.
disclaimer.accuracy: AQI forecasts are based on atmospheric models. Actual air quality may vary due to local emissions, wind patterns, and other environmental factors.
disclaimer.externalApi: Air quality and weather data is sourced from third-party APIs (Open-Meteo/CAMS). We do not guarantee accuracy or availability.

about.title: About Air Index Today
about.metaDescription: Air Index Today provides free air quality index forecasts using data from the Copernicus Atmosphere Monitoring Service (CAMS) via Open-Meteo.
about.whatIs_text: Air Index Today is a free air quality monitoring service. It shows the current US AQI (EPA standard), hourly forecasts, pollutant breakdowns (PM2.5, PM10, NO₂, O₃, SO₂, CO), a 5-day AQI trend, and health recommendations aligned with AirNow guidelines.
about.dataSources_cams: Air quality data from the Copernicus Atmosphere Monitoring Service via
about.dataSources_epa: US AQI scale, categories, and health guidance from the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA AirNow)
about.howItWorks_text: Air quality data comes from the Copernicus Atmosphere Monitoring Service (CAMS), which uses atmospheric modeling and satellite observations for air quality forecasts worldwide. Weather data (temperature, conditions) comes from the Open-Meteo forecast API.
about.free_text: Air Index Today is free to use. The service is supported by non-intrusive advertising. We do not sell personal data or require user accounts.
about.contact_text: Air Index Today is a project by

privacy.title: Privacy Policy
privacy.metaDescription: Air Index Today privacy policy. We collect no personal data, use no cookies, and require no accounts.
privacy.whoWeAre_text: This policy covers airindex.today, an air quality index forecast service operated by SimpleMeteo.
privacy.ipGeo_text: When you first visit the site, your IP address is processed server-side using the IP2Location LITE database to determine your approximate location for the air quality forecast. The IP address is not stored, logged, or transmitted to any third party.
privacy.analytics_text: We use Simple Analytics, a privacy-first analytics service based in the Netherlands (EU). It collects no personal data, sets no cookies, and does not track individual visitors.
privacy.localStorage_text: Your language and temperature unit preferences are stored in your browser's localStorage. This data never leaves your device.
privacy.airQualityApi_text: When you view an air quality forecast, your selected location's coordinates are sent to Open-Meteo and the CAMS API to retrieve air quality and weather data. No personal information is included in these requests.
privacy.whatWeDoNot_text: We do not use cookies. We do not require user accounts or authentication. We do not collect names, emails, or phone numbers. We do not track individual users across sessions. We do not sell or share any data with advertisers or third parties.
privacy.cookies_text: airindex.today sets no tracking cookies, no analytics cookies, and no advertising cookies.
privacy.thirdParty_openmeteo: Air quality and weather data provider. Receives only location coordinates. No personal data.
privacy.thirdParty_sa: Privacy-first analytics. Based in the EU. Collects no personal data. No cookies.
privacy.thirdParty_ip2: Local IP geolocation database. IP is processed server-side only, never transmitted externally.
privacy.thirdParty_cloudflare: CDN and DDoS protection. Routes traffic through its network. See Cloudflare privacy policy.
privacy.gdpr_text: Since we do not store personal data in any database, most GDPR data subject rights (access, rectification, erasure, portability) are not applicable. If you have questions, contact us via the link below.
privacy.children_text: This website is not directed at children under 16. We do not knowingly process personal data from children.
privacy.changes_text: If our data practices change materially, this page will be updated before those changes take effect.

home.hero: Air Quality Index for Any Location
home.heroSub: Current AQI, hourly forecast, pollutant levels, and health advice — free, fast, and private.
home.aqiFocusDesc: Built entirely around the air quality index — current AQI, hourly chart, pollutant breakdown, and 5-day trend at a glance.
home.fastDesc: Loads instantly on any device — no sign-up, no clutter. Get your AQI forecast in seconds, even on a slow connection.
home.privacyDesc: No cookies, no user tracking, no personal data stored. All databases are self-hosted — your location data never leaves our servers.
home.dataDesc: AQI data from the Copernicus Atmosphere Monitoring Service (CAMS) satellite system. Health guidance follows the US EPA AirNow standard.
home.scaleIntro: The AQI is a standardized index developed by the US EPA to communicate how clean or polluted the air is. It runs from 0 to 500 and is divided into six color-coded categories.
home.goodDesc: Air quality is satisfactory. It's a great day to be active outdoors.
home.moderateDesc: Acceptable. Unusually sensitive people should consider reducing prolonged outdoor exertion.
home.unhealthySensitiveDesc: Sensitive groups should reduce prolonged outdoor exertion. Take more breaks and watch for symptoms.
home.unhealthyDesc: Everyone should reduce prolonged outdoor exertion. Sensitive groups should avoid outdoor activity.
home.veryUnhealthyDesc: Health alert: everyone should avoid prolonged outdoor exertion. Move activities indoors.
home.hazardousDesc: Health warning: avoid all outdoor physical activity. Stay indoors and keep activity levels light.
home.citiesIntro: Explore the current air quality index for major cities around the world. Click any city for a detailed hourly AQI forecast and health advice.
home.continentIntro_europe: Current air quality levels for cities across Europe. From industrial centers to coastal towns — check AQI conditions before heading out.
home.continentIntro_northAmerica: Real-time air quality for cities across North America. From wildfire-prone regions to urban centers — see where AQI levels are highest.
home.continentIntro_southAmerica: Air quality overview for South American cities. Industrial and high-traffic areas can see elevated AQI — always check before outdoor activities.
home.continentIntro_asia: Current AQI levels across Asia and the Middle East. Dense urban areas and industrial regions often see unhealthy air quality.
home.continentIntro_africa: Air quality for major African cities. Dust storms, traffic, and industry can elevate pollutant levels significantly.
home.continentIntro_oceania: Air quality for cities in Australia, New Zealand, and the Pacific. Bushfire season can cause hazardous AQI spikes.
home.whatText: The Air Quality Index is calculated from concentrations of major pollutants: ground-level ozone (O₃), fine particles (PM2.5 and PM10), nitrogen dioxide (NO₂), sulfur dioxide (SO₂), and carbon monoxide (CO). Each pollutant gets its own AQI value, and the highest determines the overall index. The data comes from the Copernicus Atmosphere Monitoring Service (CAMS), which uses satellite observations and atmospheric models to forecast air quality worldwide.
home.whyMattersText: Air pollution causes respiratory issues, aggravates asthma and allergies, and increases the risk of cardiovascular problems. Fine particles (PM2.5) can penetrate deep into the lungs and bloodstream. The World Health Organization estimates that air pollution causes millions of premature deaths worldwide each year. Checking the AQI before outdoor activities helps you make informed decisions — especially if you're in a sensitive group.
home.siteDescText: Air Index Today provides free air quality forecasts for any location worldwide. Our data comes from the Copernicus Atmosphere Monitoring Service (CAMS) via Open-Meteo. We follow the US EPA AQI standard with six color-coded categories and health guidance aligned with AirNow recommendations.
